findings.summary: Settings class is a hub object coupling all modules to all configuration concerns
evidence: Settings contains 35+ attributes spanning OCR, classification, daemon, image, logging, and tag concerns
common/tags.py:clean_pipeline_tags accepts full Settings but only reads 6 tag IDs from it
common/tags.py:finalize_document_with_error accepts full Settings for the same narrow tag-ID subset
Both ocr/worker.py and classifier/worker.py pass the full Settings object through multiple layers where only a few attributes are needed
findings.suggestion: Consider narrowing function signatures to accept only the specific tag IDs or config subsets they need, or split Settings into domain-specific config groups (OcrConfig, ClassifyConfig, DaemonConfig)

findings.summary: bootstrap_daemon uses string-based getattr to access Settings attributes, blurring the contract
evidence: bootstrap_daemon takes processing_tag_id_attr='OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID' and pre_tag_id_attr='PRE_TAG_ID' as strings, then calls getattr(settings, processing_tag_id_attr)
This hides the dependency between bootstrap and specific Settings fields from static analysis and IDE navigation
findings.suggestion: Accept the actual int values directly (processing_tag_id: int | None, pre_tag_id: int) instead of attribute name strings

findings.summary: Refusal/error phrases are defined independently in both classifier/constants.py and common/config.py
evidence: classifier/constants.py defines ERROR_PHRASES tuple with 5 hardcoded phrases including 'chatgpt refused to transcribe'
common/config.py defines OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS with a similar but not identical set of phrases as configurable defaults
classifier/quality_gates.py:needs_error_tag uses the hardcoded ERROR_PHRASES via is_error_content
ocr/provider.py:is_refusal uses the configurable settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS — the two sets can drift
findings.suggestion: Consolidate refusal/error phrase definitions into a single shared source (either all configurable via Settings or all in a shared constants module)

findings.summary: ocr/__init__.py and classifier/__init__.py define __all__ exports while common/__init__.py is a bare docstring
evidence: ocr/__init__.py imports 5 symbols and defines __all__ with 6 entries
classifier/__init__.py imports 4 symbols and defines __all__ with 5 entries
common/__init__.py contains only a docstring with no imports or __all__
All consumers of common import directly from submodules (common.config, common.paperless, etc.) rather than through common/__init__.py
findings.suggestion: This is a minor inconsistency. Either add re-exports to common/__init__.py or document the convention that common is always imported by submodule

findings.summary: Stats tracking pattern differs between OcrProvider (class-level init) and ClassificationProvider (method-level reset)
evidence: OpenAIProvider.__init__ initializes _stats dict with 4 keys in __init__ and never resets it
ClassificationProvider.__init__ initializes _stats as empty dict {}, then _reset_stats() rebuilds it with 7 keys at the start of each classify_text call
Both classes have identical _inc_stat and get_stats methods but different lifecycle semantics
findings.suggestion: Align the stats pattern: either both initialize in __init__ or both reset per-call, and consider extracting a shared StatsTracker helper

findings.summary: OcrProvider ABC has only one concrete implementation (OpenAIProvider) with no evident extension pressure
evidence: OcrProvider defines abstractmethod transcribe_image and a get_stats method that returns {}
OpenAIProvider is the only subclass in the entire codebase
ocr/daemon.py hardcodes OpenAIProvider() — there is no provider selection or plugin mechanism
The docstring says 'Subclass this to add support for a new vision-capable LLM backend' but no other backend exists
findings.suggestion: Consider removing the ABC and using OpenAIProvider directly until a second provider is actually needed. The ABC adds indirection without current benefit

findings.summary: OpenAIChatMixin provides only _create_completion but requires implicit self.settings contract
evidence: OpenAIChatMixin contains a single method _create_completion that wraps openai.chat.completions.create with retry and semaphore
The mixin relies on self.settings existing on the host class (duck-typed via the retry decorator's RetrySettings protocol)
Both consumers (OpenAIProvider, ClassificationProvider) inherit it, but the implicit contract is not enforced at the type level
unique_models is a standalone function in the same module, showing the module already mixes free functions with the mixin
findings.suggestion: Replace the mixin with a plain function create_completion(settings, **kwargs) or a standalone LLMClient class that receives settings explicitly

findings.summary: Three layers of indirection for the LLM concurrency semaphore: init_llm_semaphore -> llm_limiter.init, llm_semaphore -> llm_limiter.acquire
evidence: init_llm_semaphore(max_concurrent) is a one-line wrapper that calls llm_limiter.init(max_concurrent)
llm_semaphore() context manager is a one-line wrapper that yields from llm_limiter.acquire()
Both wrapper functions exist solely to hide the module-level llm_limiter singleton, adding indirection without policy or translation
findings.suggestion: Export llm_limiter directly and let callers use llm_limiter.init() and llm_limiter.acquire(). The wrapper functions add no value over the singleton's own methods

findings.summary: Redaction/refusal detection logic is implemented in both common/utils.py and ocr/provider.py
evidence: common/utils.py:contains_redacted_marker checks for [REDACTED ...] patterns using _REDACTED_RE regex
common/utils.py:is_error_content combines refusal phrase check with redaction check
ocr/provider.py:is_refusal independently combines refusal marker check with contains_redacted_marker call
is_refusal and is_error_content do essentially the same thing (check phrases + check redaction markers) but with different interfaces and different phrase sources
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single function in common/utils.py that both OCR and classifier use, parameterized by the phrase list

findings.summary: Library setup mutates openai module globals (api_key, base_url, http_client) creating implicit shared state
evidence: library_setup.py sets openai.base_url, openai.api_key, and openai.http_client as module-level globals
common/llm.py calls openai.chat.completions.create() relying on these globals being set
common/preflight.py calls openai.models.list() relying on the same globals
This creates an implicit initialization order dependency that is invisible in import analysis
findings.suggestion: Consider creating an explicit openai.Client() instance in library_setup and passing it through, rather than mutating module-level globals. This makes the dependency explicit and testable

findings.summary: 17 bare 'except Exception:' catches swallow all errors uniformly, losing type information
evidence: claims.py lines 30, 53, 64 all catch bare Exception for three distinct API calls (refresh, update, verify) — each logs via log.exception then returns False, but the caller cannot distinguish network failure from auth error from data corruption
tags.py lines 76, 106, 120 catch bare Exception on API calls where more specific httpx.HTTPError or OSError would preserve intent

taxonomy.py lines 191, 216, 246 catch bare Exception on create_* calls — these retry by refreshing the cache, but any exception (including KeyError from malformed response) triggers the same recovery path
stale_lock.py lines 26, 53 catch bare Exception on API calls
Contrast: tags.py get_latest_tags (line 45) catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError) — showing the codebase knows how to catch specific exceptions but doesn't do so consistently
findings.suggestion: Replace bare 'except Exception:' with specific exception types (httpx.HTTPError, OSError, ValueError) at API boundaries. Reserve bare Exception catches only for the daemon_loop top-level handler where catching everything is intentional.

findings.summary: Error signaling mixes return-None, return-False, and raise across sibling functions at the same abstraction level
evidence: claim_processing_tag returns bool (True/False) to signal success/failure, swallowing exceptions
worker._refresh_document returns None on failure (catching all exceptions), while sibling paperless_client.download_content raises on failure — both in the same process() method
tags.release_processing_tag swallows all exceptions silently (returns None), while tags.finalize_document_with_error allows exceptions to propagate — yet both are called in the same finally blocks
The caller (worker.process()) must handle three different error conventions: bool return, None return, and raised exceptions, all within one method
findings.suggestion: Adopt a consistent convention at each layer: tag-lifecycle functions should either all return bool or all raise. Document which layer is expected to catch and which to propagate.

findings.summary: Tag lifecycle functions silently swallow errors via log.exception + return, masking persistent failures
evidence: tags.remove_stale_queue_tag (line 76) catches all exceptions and only logs — if Paperless is consistently rejecting updates (e.g., auth expired), this will silently fail on every poll cycle with no escalation
tags.release_processing_tag (lines 106, 120) has two separate except blocks that both swallow errors — a document could remain permanently locked if the API is unreachable
claims.claim_processing_tag has three separate except blocks (lines 30, 53, 64) all returning False — the caller sees the same signal for network error vs already-claimed
findings.suggestion: For critical operations like release_processing_tag, consider a limited retry or at minimum distinguish between transient errors (return False, will retry next cycle) and persistent errors (raise, triggers daemon-level handling).

findings.summary: Unit tests heavily patch internal module functions, coupling tests to implementation wiring
evidence: test_worker.py TestProcessHappyPath patches 4 internal functions: claim_processing_tag, bytes_to_images, assemble_full_text, release_processing_tag — changing any import path or refactoring the call chain breaks these tests without changing behavior
TestProcessErrorTagPresent patches common.tags.clean_pipeline_tags — reaching across module boundaries into internal implementation of finalize_document_with_error
TestUpdatePaperlessDocumentErrors patches both ocr.worker.get_latest_tags and common.tags.clean_pipeline_tags — two layers deep of mock setup for a single behavior test
Most unit tests use 3-5 @patch decorators per test method, creating fragile coupling to exact import paths
findings.suggestion: The e2e tests demonstrate a better pattern with stateful mock clients (_make_stateful_client). Consider using dependency injection more (the worker already accepts its dependencies in __init__) and reduce patches to external I/O boundaries only.

findings.summary: No integration tests for provider -> worker interaction with real parsing/retry logic
evidence: test_classifier_pipeline.py integration tests exercise content_prep, result parsing, tag_filters, taxonomy, and metadata — but never ClassificationProvider.classify_text with a mock OpenAI response

The provider's _create_with_compat compatibility retry logic (temperature/response_format/max_tokens stripping) is only tested in unit tests with full mocks, never with the real retry decorator and parse pipeline together
The model fallback path (trying multiple models in sequence) has no integration test verifying the full chain from provider through result parsing to tag enrichment
findings.suggestion: Add integration tests that wire ClassificationProvider (with a mocked openai.chat.completions.create) to the real result parser and tag pipeline, testing the full classify_text -> parse_classification_response -> filter chain.

findings.summary: 114 of ~830 tests (14%) are config parsing tests — disproportionate for a simple env-var loader
evidence: test_config.py has 114 test functions for Settings — more tests than the entire classifier unit test suite combined (test_provider 31 + test_worker 32 + test_result 22 = 85)
Settings is a straightforward env-var loader with no complex logic beyond int/bool parsing and validation
The classifier worker's complex apply_classification flow with 7+ steps has only 32 tests total
findings.suggestion: Consider parametrized tests for the Settings validation (one parametrized test per field type instead of individual test functions). Redirect effort toward testing the more complex classification application flow.

findings.summary: Multiple docstrings restate what is obvious from the function signature without adding insight
evidence: shutdown.py: 'Close the underlying HTTP client.' on close(self) — adds no information beyond the method name
paperless.py: 'A client for interacting with the Paperless-ngx API.' on class PaperlessClient — class name already says this
concurrency.py: 'Encapsulates a concurrency semaphore for LLM API calls.' on LLMConcurrencyLimiter — the class name and module docstring already convey this
provider.py: 'Thread-safe increment of a stats counter.' on _inc_stat — the method body is a single line with self._stats_lock
worker.py line 268: '_log_ocr_stats: Log OCR model stats.' — docstring is identical to the method name
findings.suggestion: Remove docstrings that merely restate the function name or signature. Keep docstrings that explain non-obvious behavior, edge cases, or preconditions (e.g., the retry decorator's docstring explaining self.settings requirement is valuable).

findings.summary: config.py has a 10-line module docstring and 5-line class docstring that restate obvious project structure
evidence: config.py module docstring (lines 1-11): 'This module centralizes the loading and validation of all configuration parameters from environment variables. It provides a single Settings class — a pure data container with no side effects on import — that is consumed by every other module.' — this is what every config module does

utils.py module docstring (lines 1-12): includes RST directive-style documentation ('- :func:`contains_redacted_marker` — detects...') for a module with only 2 functions — the function docstrings already cover this

config.py class docstring (lines 19-25): 'A container for all configuration settings, loaded from environment variables. This class centralizes configuration, providing default values...' — repeats the module docstring
findings.suggestion: Trim module docstrings to one line summaries. The RST-style function listings in utils.py are unnecessary for a 2-function module.

findings.summary: Identical stats tracking boilerplate duplicated verbatim between OCR and classification providers
evidence: ocr/provider.py OpenAIProvider.__init__: self._stats_lock = threading.Lock(); self._stats = {'attempts': 0, 'refusals': 0, ...}
classifier/provider.py ClassificationProvider.__init__: self._stats = {}; self._stats_lock = threading.Lock()
Both have identical _inc_stat and get_stats methods: 'with self._stats_lock: self._stats[key] += 1' and 'with self._stats_lock: return dict(self._stats)'
The stats pattern (lock + dict + inc + get) is repeated verbatim — a hallmark of copy-paste generation
findings.suggestion: Extract a simple ThreadSafeStats class into common/ that both providers can compose, eliminating the duplicated _inc_stat / get_stats / _stats_lock boilerplate.

findings.summary: Heavy use of section-divider comments and block headers in small modules where they add no navigational value
evidence: tags.py has 4 section dividers ('# -----------...\n# Tag extraction\n# -----------...') for a 183-line file with 6 functions — every other function gets a banner
tag_filters.py has 4 section dividers for 5 functions in 191 lines
These dividers are characteristic of LLM-generated code that over-structures small files
findings.suggestion: Remove section dividers in files under 200 lines where the functions are already well-named and logically ordered. Keep them only in larger files where visual navigation genuinely helps.

findings.summary: Three-package layout (common, ocr, classifier) is well-aligned with domain boundaries and dependency direction
evidence: common/ (15 files) contains genuinely shared infrastructure: config, paperless client, retry, tags, daemon_loop, shutdown — all imported by both ocr/ and classifier/
ocr/ (7 files) and classifier/ (13 files) depend on common/ but never on each other — clean unidirectional dependency
Both domain packages follow identical structural patterns: daemon.py (entry point), worker.py (orchestrator), provider.py (LLM interface)
classifier/ has additional modules (taxonomy, tag_filters, normalizers, content_prep, metadata, quality_gates, constants) reflecting its greater complexity — appropriate decomposition
findings.suggestion: No action needed. The layout is well-organized for a project of this size.

findings.summary: common/utils.py is a small catch-all with only 2 functions that could live in more specific locations
evidence: utils.py contains contains_redacted_marker() and is_error_content() — both are text-content analysis functions
contains_redacted_marker is imported by ocr/provider.py and ocr/worker.py; is_error_content is imported only by classifier/quality_gates.py
The module name 'utils' is generic — 'content_checks' or similar would be more descriptive
However, at 60 lines total this is a minor concern and the functions are genuinely shared
findings.suggestion: Consider renaming utils.py to content_checks.py for discoverability. Low priority given the small size.

findings.summary: The three-package decomposition (common, ocr, classifier) maps cleanly to domain capabilities with clear ownership boundaries
evidence: common/ contains shared infrastructure (config, paperless client, retry, tags, daemon loop), ocr/ contains vision-LLM transcription, classifier/ contains document classification - each package has a coherent purpose
findings.suggestion: No change needed - this is a positive observation

findings.summary: Settings class mixes OCR-specific, classifier-specific, and shared config into a single 80+ field monolith
evidence: Settings has CLASSIFY_* fields (8 fields), OCR_* fields (4 fields), and shared fields all in one class; both daemons load all config regardless of which one is running; the class spans 250 lines with 7 private loader methods
findings.suggestion: Consider splitting into BaseSettings + OcrSettings + ClassifierSettings, or at minimum group with nested dataclasses to reflect the domain ownership boundaries

findings.summary: bootstrap_daemon uses stringly-typed getattr to access settings attributes, obscuring the contract
evidence: recover_stale_locks call uses getattr(settings, processing_tag_id_attr) and getattr(settings, pre_tag_id_attr) where the callers pass string attribute names like 'OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID' - this loses type safety and makes the dependency invisible
findings.suggestion: Accept the tag IDs directly as int parameters instead of attribute name strings, letting each daemon pass its specific values

findings.summary: Each document creates a new PaperlessClient (HTTP session) instead of reusing the listing client
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 25: PaperlessClient(settings) created per-document in _process_document; classifier/daemon.py line 67: same pattern; the list_client is already available and thread-safe via httpx.Client
findings.suggestion: httpx.Client is thread-safe; consider reusing the existing client or using a connection pool instead of constructing a new HTTP session per document

findings.summary: OpenAIProvider and ClassificationProvider duplicate the stats tracking pattern (lock, dict, _inc_stat, get_stats) with no shared abstraction
evidence: ocr/provider.py lines 83-101: _stats_lock, _stats dict, _inc_stat, get_stats; classifier/provider.py lines 28-56: identical pattern with _stats_lock, _stats dict, _inc_stat, get_stats; both use threading.Lock with the same increment/snapshot logic
findings.suggestion: Extract a small StatsCounter helper into common/ or add the stats tracking to OpenAIChatMixin since both providers inherit from it

findings.summary: The retry decorator silently requires self.settings to satisfy RetrySettings protocol, creating an invisible coupling
evidence: retry.py line 33: 'settings: RetrySettings = self.settings' assumes the decorated method's owner has a settings attribute; this contract is documented in a docstring but not enforced or visible at the call site; PaperlessClient and OpenAIChatMixin both silently depend on this
findings.suggestion: Consider making the retry settings explicit via a parameter or a base class/protocol that declares the settings attribute

findings.summary: OcrProvider ABC has only one concrete implementation (OpenAIProvider) and no extension pressure
evidence: OcrProvider ABC defined at line 46 with abstractmethod transcribe_image; only OpenAIProvider implements it; no plugin system or factory; the docstring says 'Subclass this to add support for a new vision-capable LLM backend' but no second backend exists
findings.suggestion: The ABC adds minimal overhead here, but if no second provider is planned it could be simplified to just OpenAIProvider

findings.summary: PaperlessClient._create_named_item has convoluted matching_algorithm candidate logic for a simple compatibility concern
evidence: Lines 63-100: builds a candidates list with None/str/int permutations, then loops through them catching 400 errors; the branching on isinstance(matching_algorithm, str) vs int with different fallback orders is hard to follow; the method also has an implicit non-return path (if last_response is not checked after the loop)
findings.suggestion: Simplify by always trying both representations in a fixed order, or handle the compatibility at a higher level (TaxonomyCache._infer_matching_algorithm already does this)

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._create_with_compat has a repetitive if/elif/elif ladder for stripping parameters on 400 errors
evidence: Lines 104-149: three nearly identical blocks each check a predicate, log a warning, increment a stat, pop a param, increment compat_retries, and continue; the only differences are the param name, the predicate method, and the stat key
findings.suggestion: Extract a list of (predicate, param_name, stat_key) tuples and iterate over them, reducing ~30 lines to ~10

findings.summary: Both worker process() methods combine refresh, validation, lock acquisition, processing, and cleanup in a single long method with deep nesting
evidence: ocr/worker.py process() spans lines 53-131 with nested try/finally blocks and multiple early returns; classifier/worker.py process() spans lines 70-142 with similar structure; both have the pattern: try → refresh → check error → claim → [domain work] → finally release
findings.suggestion: The shared try/claim/finally pattern could be extracted into a context manager or template method in common/, letting workers focus on just the domain-specific steps

findings.summary: Tag set manipulation (add/discard/clean pipeline tags) is spread across multiple callsites with no centralized state machine
evidence: ocr/worker.py lines 231-234: manual discard PRE, discard PROCESSING, add POST; classifier/worker.py lines 296-299: similar manual tag arithmetic; common/tags.py clean_pipeline_tags removes a hardcoded list of tag IDs; finalize_document_with_error does its own version; the tag lifecycle transitions are implicit and easy to get wrong
findings.suggestion: Introduce a TagTransition helper or state machine that encodes the valid transitions (queued -> processing -> done, queued -> error) and applies them atomically

findings.summary: OCR refusal markers are defined in both Settings (OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS) and classifier/constants.py (ERROR_PHRASES) with overlapping content
evidence: config.py lines 113-119: default_ocr_refusal_markers includes 'i can't assist', 'chatgpt refused to transcribe'; constants.py lines 55-61: ERROR_PHRASES includes the same phrases; these are checked independently by ocr/provider.py is_refusal() and classifier/quality_gates.py needs_error_tag()
findings.suggestion: Define the canonical refusal phrases once in common/ and let both pipelines reference the same source; the configurable OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS can extend (not duplicate) the base set
